column1: What separates the frontal from parietal lobe?
column2: Central sulcus (Rolando)

column1: What separates the frontal from temporal lobe?
column2: Lateral sulcus (Sylvian)

column1: Broca area is in which gyrus and lobe?
column2: Inferior frontal gyrus, Frontal lobe (Brodmann 44, 45)

column1: Wernicke area is in which gyrus and lobe?
column2: Superior temporal gyrus, Temporal lobe (Brodmann 22)

column1: What are the components of the basal ganglia?
column2: Caudate nucleus, Putamen, Globus pallidus (internus/externus), Subthalamic nucleus, Substantia nigra (pars compacta + pars reticulata)

column1: Which layer of neocortex receives thalamic input?
column2: Layer IV — Internal granular layer

column1: Which layer contains Betz cells (corticofugal output to spinal cord)?
column2: Layer V — Internal pyramidal layer

column1: What is the primary function of the caudate nucleus?
column2: Motor planning and procedural memory; degeneration causes Huntington disease

column1: STN lesion causes what movement disorder?
column2: Hemiballismus — violent, flinging movements of contralateral limbs

column1: What are the 6 layers of the neocortex (superficial to deep)?
column2: I Molecular, II External granular, III External pyramidal, IV Internal granular, V Internal pyramidal, VI Multiform

column1: What percentage of cortex is hidden within sulci?
column2: Two-thirds (2/3) is within sulci; one-third exposed on gyri

column1: What is the blood supply of Broca and Wernicke areas?
column2: Usually LEFT hemisphere: MCA (middle cerebral artery) territory

column1: Gerstmann syndrome localizes to which gyrus?
column2: Angular gyrus of the dominant hemisphere (usually left) — Brodmann 39

column1: What separates the parietal from occipital lobe?
column2: Parieto-occipital sulcus

column1: What is the precentral gyrus function?
column2: Primary motor cortex (M1) — Brodmann area 4 — voluntary motor control

column1: What is the postcentral gyrus function?
column2: Primary somatosensory cortex (S1) — Brodmann areas 3, 1, 2 — sensory input from body

column1: Direct pathway effect on movement?
column2: Facilitates movement — cortex excites striatum → inhibits GPi → disinhibits thalamus → excites cortex

column1: Indirect pathway effect on movement?
column2: Inhibits movement — cortex excites striatum → activates GPi → inhibits thalamus → inhibits cortex

column1: Direct pathway of basal ganglia — effect on movement?
column2: Facilitates movement (Go)

column1: Indirect pathway of basal ganglia — effect on movement?
column2: Suppresses movement (Stop)

column1: D1 receptor location and effect?
column2: Striatum; excites direct pathway → facilitates movement

column1: D2 receptor location and effect?
column2: Striatum; inhibits indirect pathway → disinhibits movement

column1: Parkinson disease — what degenerates?
column2: Substantia nigra pars compacta

column1: Hemiballismus — lesion of which structure?
column2: Contralateral subthalamic nucleus

column1: Huntington disease — what is seen on imaging?
column2: Caudate nucleus atrophy, ex vacuo hydrocephalus

column1: DBS target for Parkinson — two options?
column2: GPi (globus pallidus internus) or STN (subthalamic nucleus)

column1: What separates caudate from putamen?
column2: Internal capsule

column1: Parkinson — why is there too much GPi/SNr activity?
column2: Loss of dopamine → disinhibition of indirect pathway → STN overactive → GPi overactive

column1: SNr stands for?
column2: Substantia nigra pars reticulata

column1: Thalamus receives input from which basal ganglia structures?
column2: GPi and SNr

column1: Two major outputs of basal ganglia to thalamus?
column2: GPi → VA/VL thalamus; SNr → VA/VL thalamus

column1: Huntington disease — genetic mutation?
column2: CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in huntingtin gene

column1: Huntington — triad?
column2: Chorea + psychiatric symptoms + cognitive decline
